summary: Hotz argues that AGI should be defined by its ability to take almost any human job, which requires human-like embodiment and the crucial ability to learn on-device in real-world conditions. He dismisses simulation-based approaches as insufficient because simulators can never capture all real-world edge cases like bumps, gravel, or banana peels. The path forward requires robots that learn live on hardware, similar to how Berkeley got a robot dog walking in 20 minutes of real-world training. He explores the human reward function as a multi-part meta-system encoded in DNA, with components like autoregression, curiosity, and behavioral cloning that must be captured for true AGI.
key_points: AGI should be defined as a machine capable of taking almost any human job, requiring human-like form factor, senses, and ability to interoperate with humans
Simulation-based learning is fundamentally limited because you can't anticipate every real-world edge case - on-device learning is the only viable path forward
The human reward function is learned but has an underlying 'meta reward function' encoded in DNA that needs to be captured
Learning progression must go from hand-coded basics to supervised learning to reinforcement learning with real agents - you can't skip steps
Embodiment enables mirror neurons and learning by watching humans, which is essential for human-like capability acquisition
money_quotes: Before asking the question, how do I build AGI, you first must ask the question, what would I recognize as AGI?
I define AGI as a machine capable of taking (almost) any human job.
But what happens when we encounter a bump? Gravel? Snow? A human trying to knock it over? A banana peel? Did you put all those things in your simulator?
The only way forward is on device learning.
Unlike simulation, 'resetting' can be much more expensive in the real world, so you'll need to take this into account. There are bad states, and there are BAD states.
There's not even a way to specify the complete reward function for driving, never mind for life.
Perhaps the first two are combined to maximize the derivative of compression.
key_insight: True AGI requires on-device learning in the real world because no simulator can capture the infinite edge cases of physical reality, and the human reward function itself is too complex to fully specify in advance.
